title: The management of severe isolated traumatic brain injury in pregnancy: A joint consensus statement from the European Association of Neurosurgical Societies (EANS) and the World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES)
one_line_summary: This joint EANS/WSES guideline provides 36 strong suggestions for managing severe TBI in pregnancy, balancing aggressive maternal neuroprotection with fetal viability, addressing complex physiological conflicts and historical evidence gaps.
key_pearls: Perform immediate brain CT without abdominal shielding for severe TBI in pregnancy, as diagnostic delays are unacceptable.
Maintain maternal ICP < 20-22 mmHg and CPP 60-70 mmHg, zeroing the arterial transducer at the tragus for accurate readings.
After 20-24 weeks gestation, position the patient with a 15° left lateral tilt or manual uterine displacement to prevent aortocaval compression.
Target maternal PaO2 80-120 mmHg; individualize PaCO2 but avoid aggressive hyperventilation (<32 mmHg) without advanced neuromonitoring.
Use hypertonic saline/mannitol cautiously for ICP; keep maternal Na < 155 mEq/L and Osm < 320 mOsm/kg to prevent fetal dehydration.
Levetiracetam is the preferred AED for seizure prophylaxis; avoid teratogenic agents like valproate, phenobarbital, and phenytoin.
For maternal cardiac arrest at >= 20-24 weeks gestation, perform resuscitative hysterotomy within 4-5 minutes at the bedside.
consensus_method: modified Delphi consensus methodology combined with a systematic scoping review

recommendation_blocks.narrative: The management of gestational TBI requires immediate, highly coordinated care. The consensus strongly advocates that pregnant patients with severe isolated TBI be admitted exclusively to tertiary care facilities equipped with 24/7 neurosurgical, maternal-fetal medicine (MFM), and neonatal intensive care units (NICU). This infrastructure is mandatory to manage the dual-patient dynamic, particularly as the fetus approaches the threshold of viability (typically 22-24 weeks). A multidisciplinary "brain-obstetric" team must be activated immediately upon admission to prevent fragmented care and ensure that maternal resuscitation does not inadvertently compromise the fetus.

recommendations.statement: Admit pregnant patients with severe isolated TBI exclusively to tertiary care facilities with 24/7 neurosurgical, MFM, and NICU capabilities.
Activate a multidisciplinary 'brain-obstetric' team immediately upon admission.
Maintain awareness of pregnancy-specific physiology during management.
Employ a multidisciplinary and ethical approach to controversial clinical scenarios.
Implement specific hospital protocols for the management of severe TBI in pregnancy.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: In the hyperacute phase of severe TBI, diagnostic delays due to fear of fetal ionizing radiation exposure are unacceptable. The panel recommends immediate brain computed tomography (CT) scanning, with or without intravenous contrast, identical to the protocol for non-pregnant patients. Crucially, the consensus recommends *against* abdominal shielding during brain CT. Modern CT scanners utilize automatic exposure control; placing a lead shield over the abdomen can interfere with these algorithms, paradoxically increasing the scatter radiation dose and degrading image quality. Furthermore, the primary source of fetal radiation during a head CT is internal scatter, which shielding cannot prevent. Outside the acute, life-threatening window, non-contrast brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is preferred. Gadolinium-based contrast agents must be avoided due to documented teratogenic risks in animal models, unless the maternal diagnostic benefit absolutely outweighs the fetal risk.

recommendations.statement: Perform immediate brain CT scanning, with or without intravenous contrast, identical to non-pregnant patients.
Do not use abdominal shielding during brain CT scanning.
Prefer non-contrast brain MRI for non-acute phases; avoid gadolinium-based contrast agents unless maternal benefit absolutely outweighs fetal risk.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Indications for invasive ICP monitoring in pregnant patients are identical to those in non-pregnant patients (e.g., Glasgow Coma Scale score <= 8 with abnormal brain CT). The consensus supports standard neurotrauma targets: ICP < 20-22 mmHg and CPP between 60-70 mmHg, ideally guided by cerebral autoregulation testing. To ensure accurate CPP calculations, the arterial blood pressure transducer must be zeroed at the level of the tragus (external auditory meatus) rather than the right atrium, preventing underestimation of cerebral perfusion when the head of the bed is elevated. In patients lacking invasive ICP monitoring, systemic hemodynamics must be maintained aggressively to prevent secondary ischemic brain injury, targeting a Systolic Arterial Blood Pressure (SABP) >= 110 mmHg or a Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) >= 80 mmHg. Non-invasive neuromonitoring tools—such as optic nerve sheath diameter (ONSD) ultrasonography, transcranial Doppler (TCD) cerebral blood flow velocity, and automated pupillometry (Neurological Pupillary Index, NPi)—should be utilized as screening adjuncts (using the B-ICONIC multimodal framework requiring at least two concordant modalities) but must not replace invasive monitors.

recommendations.statement: Utilize invasive ICP monitoring in pregnant patients with indications identical to non-pregnant patients (e.g., GCS <= 8 with abnormal brain CT).
Target ICP < 20-22 mmHg and CPP between 60-70 mmHg, ideally guided by cerebral autoregulation testing.
Zero the arterial blood pressure transducer at the level of the tragus for accurate CPP calculations.
In patients without ICP monitoring, target SABP >= 110 mmHg or MAP >= 80 mmHg to prevent secondary ischemic brain injury.
Utilize non-invasive neuromonitoring tools (TCD, ONSD, pupillometry) as screening adjuncts, but not as replacements for invasive monitors.
Employ the B-ICONIC multimodal framework, requiring at least two concordant non-invasive modalities for screening.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Optimizing cerebral venous drainage is vital to control ICP. The head of the bed must be maintained at 30 to 45 degrees, with the head kept strictly in the midline position to prevent jugular vein compression. However, after mid-pregnancy (when the gravid uterus reaches or exceeds the level of the umbilicus, typically at 20-24 weeks' gestation), the patient must be positioned with a left lateral tilt of approximately 15 degrees, or undergo manual left uterine displacement. This maneuver is critical to alleviate aortocaval compression by the gravid uterus, which can severely reduce venous return, maternal cardiac output, and uterine blood flow. If a concomitant traumatic spinal cord injury is suspected, spinal alignment must be meticulously maintained during tilting.

recommendations.statement: Maintain the head of the bed at 30 to 45 degrees.
Keep the head strictly in the midline position to prevent jugular vein compression.
After mid-pregnancy (>= 20-24 weeks gestation), position the patient with a left lateral tilt of approximately 15 degrees or perform manual left uterine displacement.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Maintaining adequate systemic oxygenation is paramount; the panel suggests targeting an arterial partial pressure of oxygen (PaO2) of 80-120 mmHg (adjusted for altitude), with higher levels permitted if guided by advanced neuromonitoring (such as brain tissue oxygen tension, PbtO2) and continuous fetal monitoring. Carbon dioxide management is highly complex in pregnancy. While pregnant women naturally exhibit a physiological respiratory alkalosis (with a baseline PaCO2 of 26-30 mmHg due to progesterone-driven hyperventilation), there is no evidence regarding the safety of these low levels in the acutely injured brain. Hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction, which can worsen regional brain ischemia. Therefore, the consensus recommends that PaCO2 targets be individualized based on invasive ICP monitoring, PbtO2, and fetal heart rate monitoring. In the absence of advanced neuromonitoring, aggressive hyperventilation (PaCO2 < 32 mmHg) should be avoided.

recommendations.statement: Target an arterial partial pressure of oxygen (PaO2) of 80-120 mmHg (adjusted for altitude).
Individualize PaCO2 targets based on invasive ICP monitoring, PbtO2, and fetal heart rate monitoring; avoid aggressive hyperventilation (PaCO2 < 32 mmHg) without advanced neuromonitoring.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Gestational TBI is frequently complicated by trauma-induced coagulopathy, which increases the risk of hemorrhagic progression. In patients requiring emergency neurosurgery, invasive ICP monitor placement, or those with evolving hemorrhagic lesions, the panel recommends maintaining a platelet count > 75,000/mm3 and a prothrombin time (PT) / activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) < 1.5 times the normal control. Viscoelastic point-of-care tests (TEG or ROTEM) should be utilized to guide targeted factor replacement. However, clinicians must avoid overcorrecting coagulation parameters, as pregnancy is a physiologically hypercoagulable state, placing these patients at an elevated risk for venous thromboembolism (VTE). Mechanical VTE prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) devices must be initiated immediately upon admission. Pharmacological thromboprophylaxis (low-molecular-weight heparin) should be considered within 24-72 hours post-injury, provided that follow-up head imaging demonstrates hemorrhagic stability and a multidisciplinary consensus (including neurosurgery and MFM) is reached. Transfusion thresholds should mirror those of non-pregnant patients (typically targeting a hemoglobin level > 7-9 g/dL), guided by maternal cardiorespiratory status and fetal well-being.

recommendations.statement: Mirror transfusion thresholds of non-pregnant patients (Hb > 7-9 g/dL), guided by maternal cardiorespiratory status and fetal well-being.
Maintain a platelet count > 75,000/mm3 in patients requiring neurosurgery, ICP monitoring, or with evolving hemorrhagic lesions.
Maintain PT/aPTT < 1.5 times the normal control.
Utilize viscoelastic point-of-care tests (TEG or ROTEM) to guide targeted factor replacement.
Initiate mechanical VTE prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) devices immediately upon admission.
Consider pharmacological thromboprophylaxis (LMWH) within 24-72 hours post-injury if head imaging is stable and multidisciplinary consensus is reached.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Osmotherapy is a cornerstone of ICP management, but its use in pregnancy requires extreme caution. Hypertonic saline or mannitol may be used to control intracranial hypertension, but serum sodium must be kept < 155 mEq/L and serum osmolality < 320 mOsm/kg. Excessive maternal hypernatremia and hyperosmolality can cause rapid placental water transfer, leading to severe fetal dehydration. This can manifest as oligohydramnios, fetal growth restriction, and late decelerations on fetal heart rate monitoring. Strict volume status monitoring is required to maintain normovolemia. Regarding glycemic control, both hypoglycemia and extreme hyperglycemia are highly detrimental to the injured brain. The panel suggests maintaining maternal blood glucose levels between 100 and 180 mg/dL. While some obstetric guidelines for gestational diabetes recommend tighter control (upper limit of 140-150 mg/dL), the panel cautions that overly aggressive insulin therapy increases the risk of maternal hypoglycemia, which can cause catastrophic secondary brain injury.

recommendations.statement: Use hypertonic saline or mannitol for ICP control, but keep serum sodium < 155 mEq/L and serum osmolality < 320 mOsm/kg to avoid fetal dehydration.
Maintain maternal blood glucose levels between 100 and 180 mg/dL, avoiding overly aggressive insulin therapy.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: For patients with refractory intracranial hypertension, a stepwise therapeutic escalation is recommended. The maintenance of normothermia (core body temperature between 36.0 and 37.5 degrees C) is the standard of care. If targeted temperature management (TTM) is utilized for ICP control, the core body temperature should not be allowed to fall below 36.0 degrees C, as induced hypothermia can trigger severe fetal bradycardia and arrhythmias. If lower temperatures are clinically unavoidable as a life-saving measure for the mother, continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is mandatory. Barbiturate coma, historically used as a tier-three therapy for refractory ICP, should be avoided if possible. Barbiturates cause profound maternal hemodynamic instability, immunosuppression, and are associated with fetal malformations. If first- and second-tier therapies fail, secondary decompressive craniectomy (DC) is strongly preferred over barbiturate coma, as it provides rapid surgical decompression without the systemic and teratogenic side effects of high-dose sedatives.

recommendations.statement: Employ a stepwise therapeutic escalation for refractory intracranial hypertension.
Maintain normothermia (core body temperature between 36.0 and 37.5 degrees C).
Avoid barbiturate coma; strongly prefer secondary decompressive craniectomy (DC) for refractory ICP if first- and second-tier therapies fail.
Avoid targeted temperature management below 36.0 degrees C due to risk of fetal bradycardia; if unavoidable, continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is mandatory.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Post-traumatic seizures can cause acute spikes in ICP, massive metabolic stress, and maternal hypoxia, all of which are highly detrimental to both the mother and the fetus. Seizure prophylaxis should be initiated in pregnant patients with severe TBI using the same clinical indications as non-pregnant cohorts (typically for the first 7 days post-injury). However, the choice of antiepileptic drug (AED) must be carefully tailored. Classic AEDs with well-documented fetal teratogenicity—such as valproic acid, phenobarbital, and phenytoin—must be avoided, particularly during the first trimester. Levetiracetam is the preferred first-line agent due to its favorable safety profile, lack of teratogenicity, and minimal drug-drug interactions. Immediate consultation with a neurologist is advised to optimize dosing, especially since physiological increases in glomerular filtration rate during pregnancy can lower serum AED levels.

recommendations.statement: Initiate seizure prophylaxis in pregnant patients with severe TBI using the same clinical indications as non-pregnant cohorts (typically for 7 days).
Avoid teratogenic AEDs (valproic acid, phenobarbital, phenytoin); prefer levetiracetam as the first-line agent.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: In the catastrophic event of maternal cardiac arrest, immediate and decisive action is required. If the pregnant patient is at or beyond a gestational age of 20-24 weeks, or if the fundus of the uterus is visible above the umbilicus, a resuscitative hysterotomy (perimortem cesarean section) must be performed immediately at the bedside. The procedure should ideally be completed within 4 to 5 minutes of cardiac arrest. This serves a dual purpose: first, it relieves aortocaval compression, immediately improving venous return and the efficacy of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) for the mother; second, it offers the only viable chance of survival for a viable fetus. Resuscitative efforts for both the mother and the newborn must continue concurrently post-delivery.

recommendations.statement: Perform resuscitative hysterotomy immediately at the bedside within 4-5 minutes of maternal cardiac arrest if gestational age is >= 20-24 weeks or the uterus is above the umbilicus.
Ensure immediate MFM involvement for fetal monitoring.

bedside_protocol.title: Admission & Initial Setup
bedside_protocol.action: Activate a multidisciplinary team including Neurosurgery, MFM, ICU, OB/GYN, and Neonatology. Transfer the patient to a tertiary center equipped with 24/7 specialized units. Perform an immediate brain CT scan without abdominal shielding.

bedside_protocol.title: Hyperacute Resuscitation & Stabilization
bedside_protocol.action: Target PaO2 80-120 mmHg; PaCO2 35-40 mmHg (unless ICP monitored). Maintain SABP >= 110 mmHg or MAP >= 80 mmHg if no ICP monitor. Position the head of bed 30-45°, head midline. If gestational age >= 20-24 weeks, apply a 15° left lateral tilt. Obtain immediate MFM consult for fetal viability and continuous CTG.

bedside_protocol.title: Neurocritical Care & ICP Management
bedside_protocol.action: If indicated, insert an invasive ICP monitor, targeting ICP < 20-22 mmHg and CPP 60-70 mmHg. Zero the arterial transducer at the level of the tragus. Employ stepwise ICP escalation: Tier 1 (sedation, CSF drainage, mild hyperventilation PaCO2 32-35 mmHg); Tier 2 (osmotherapy); Tier 3 (refractory ICP, prefer secondary decompressive craniectomy over barbiturate coma, avoid hypothermia < 36.0°C).

bedside_protocol.title: Metabolic & Coagulation Management
bedside_protocol.action: Maintain maternal blood glucose 100-180 mg/dL and core temperature 36.0-37.5°C. Target platelets > 75,000/mm³ and PT/aPTT < 1.5x control. Initiate mechanical DVT prophylaxis with IPCs immediately. Consider pharmacological DVT prophylaxis with LMWH within 24-72 hours if head CT is stable.

bedside_protocol.title: Emergency Cardiac Arrest Workflow
bedside_protocol.action: If gestational age >= 20-24 weeks (or uterus above umbilicus), perform bedside resuscitative hysterotomy within 4-5 minutes of maternal cardiac arrest.

bedside_protocol.title: Osmotherapy Titration
bedside_protocol.action: Administer hypertonic saline (e.g., 3% NaCl 150 mL bolus) or mannitol (0.5-1.0 g/kg) for acute ICP spikes > 22 mmHg lasting > 5 minutes. Check serum sodium and osmolality every 4-6 hours. Discontinue osmotic boluses if serum sodium reaches 155 mEq/L, serum osmolality reaches 320 mOsm/kg, or if continuous fetal monitoring reveals persistent late decelerations or loss of variability.

bedside_protocol.title: Ventilation Titration
bedside_protocol.action: Adjust minute ventilation to maintain PaCO2 at 35-40 mmHg. If ICP is elevated, PaCO2 can be titrated down to 32-35 mmHg. Do not titrate PaCO2 < 32 mmHg unless invasive ICP and PbtO2 monitors are in place and fetal heart rate is stable. If PbtO2 drops < 20 mmHg or fetal distress is noted, immediately liberalize ventilation.

bedside_protocol.title: Glycemic Control
bedside_protocol.action: Initiate insulin infusion if blood glucose exceeds 180 mg/dL. Target a conservative range of 100-180 mg/dL. Stop insulin infusion and administer dextrose if blood glucose drops < 100 mg/dL to prevent maternal neuroglycopenia.

bedside_protocol.title: Seizure Prophylaxis
bedside_protocol.action: Administer Levetiracetam 500-1500 mg IV every 12 hours for 7 days post-injury. Monitor serum levels closely due to increased renal clearance in pregnancy.

strengths_limitations: Strengths include multidisciplinary breadth with 115 international experts across 9 specialties, a structured Delphi process with two rounds of anonymous voting facilitated by an independent methodologist, and a pragmatic clinical focus bridging standard neurocritical care with obstetric/neonatal priorities. Limitations are that recommendations are based entirely on expert opinion and low-level retrospective data (no randomized controlled trials exist). The consensus assumes a high-income country setting with immediate, 24/7 access to neurosurgery, MFM, NICU, and advanced neuromonitoring, which may not be generalizable to low-resource settings. Additionally, the B-ICONIC framework for non-invasive ICP screening has not been formally validated in pregnant cohorts.
